# GitHub Copilot / AI Agent Instructions for this repo

This is a single-package Flutter application. The guidance below highlights project-specific structure, important files, and developer workflows an AI coding agent should follow to be productive immediately.

1) Big picture
- Type: Flutter app (multi-platform). App entry: `lib/main.dart`.
- App code lives under `lib/`. Platform integration and build wrappers live under `android/`, `ios/`, `macos/`, `linux/`, `windows/`, and `web/`.
- Generated artifacts live under `build/` — do not edit files there.

2) Key files to inspect when changing behavior
- [lib/main.dart](lib/main.dart) — application entry and top-level routing.
- [pubspec.yaml](pubspec.yaml) — dependencies, SDK constraint (Dart ^3.11.4), assets.
- [test/widget_test.dart](test/widget_test.dart) — example test; use `flutter test` to run.
- Android Kotlin DSL: [android/build.gradle.kts](android/build.gradle.kts) and [android/app/build.gradle.kts](android/app/build.gradle.kts).
- iOS Swift entry: [ios/Runner/AppDelegate.swift](ios/Runner/AppDelegate.swift) and generated plugin glue in [ios/Runner/GeneratedPluginRegistrant.*](ios/Runner/GeneratedPluginRegistrant.m)

3) Build / run / test workflows (minimal, copyable)
- Install deps: `flutter pub get`
- Run locally (connect device or simulator): `flutter run -d <device-id>`
- Build Android APK: `flutter build apk`
- Build iOS: `flutter build ios` (or open Xcode: `ios/Runner.xcworkspace` for signing)
- Run tests: `flutter test`
- Android Gradle tasks: use Gradle wrapper under `android/` (Kotlin DSL): `cd android && ./gradlew assembleDebug`

4) Project-specific conventions & gotchas
- Android uses Kotlin DSL (`*.kts`) for Gradle — modifications must use Kotlin script syntax.
- iOS uses Swift for AppDelegate and has a bridging header. Generated plugin registrant files are autogenerated; avoid editing them directly.
- The repo uses the default `flutter_lints` rules via `analysis_options.yaml` — adhere to those lints when changing Dart code.
- Keep `pubspec.yaml` as the single source of Dart/Flutter dependency changes. After edits run `flutter pub get` and a full rebuild when native plugins are added.

5) Integration points and cross-component communication
- No custom platform channels located in `lib/` (search for `MethodChannel` returned no results). Platform integration is handled via standard plugin system and the generated registrant in `android/` and `ios/`.
- Plugins: adding a plugin requires updating `pubspec.yaml` and rebuilding; the tooling updates `GeneratedPluginRegistrant` for each platform.

6) Tests and CI hints
- Unit/widget tests live in `test/` and run with `flutter test`.
- Avoid relying on device-specific tests in CI; use emulators only when explicitly configured.

7) Editing guidance for AI agents
- Prefer edits under `lib/`, `test/`, and `pubspec.yaml`.
- Do not modify generated or build outputs in `build/`, `ios/Runner/GeneratedPluginRegistrant.*`, or files under platform `build/` directories.
- When changing native platform configs, mention the target platform and file (e.g., update Android Gradle Kotlin DSL in [android/app/build.gradle.kts](android/app/build.gradle.kts)).

8) Examples to copy when producing patches
- Small Dart change: update `lib/main.dart` and run `flutter test`.
- Add dependency: update `pubspec.yaml`, run `flutter pub get`, then `flutter clean` + `flutter build` if native.

9) Questions to ask the maintainer (when uncertain)
- Should changes require platform signing (iOS) or keystore updates (Android)?
- Is backward compatibility with older Flutter/Dart SDKs required?

If anything here is unclear or you want more detail (CI rules, release process, or coding conventions specific to UI/state management), tell me what to expand and I will update this file.
